speaker: interviewee
text: --artist. I don't know much about him, but this is, again, left for the research. Here is a photograph of Ryback. And the painting is Haman-- Haman-- from a Purim spiel, a play of Purim. Interesting to look at his hat. He has this swastika on his hat. It was painted in 1925, but Hitler came to power in 1920-- wrote his in 1924. On the right, there is a catalogue by Ryback. And on the left is one of the paintings that show how he was influenced by the cubists. This is [? Israel ?] [? Regenbaum ?]. In our envelope, we have one bio, one write up, and two letters-- one of them you see from 1930, probably-- and another article, and 10 photos. So the photos, we brought you only two. One of them is a photograph of the artist. And the other one is in the Yeshiva, The letter is written in German. This is a letter by Rynecki. Look at his handwriting. And we have in our collection 3 letter-- 4 letters from '30 and '31, and 18 photos. From the photos we are going to see some a minute later. It was written in Yiddish. Two photographs by Rynecki. The-- that one that you see is And the happiness, and the devotion, and the closeness to the Sefer Torah, to the book of the-- to the scroll of the Torah, is felt here. And the other one is a synagogue. Two more paintings by Rynecki. He's interested in the working people. You see on top, a carpenter's store, with the carpenter working very hard, and shavings of wood are on the floor. And below, you see the wassertrager, the two people who are carrying water from house to house. Leopold Gottlieb was a very famous artist. We have from him two letters that I found. I didn't find any photographs, but we have a bunch of photographs that I couldn't identify. And perhaps some of them belong to Leopold Gottlieb. He wrote it-- he wrote this letter in 1931, in German, from Paris. This letter is also from Leopold Gottlieb, also from Paris, in 1930. This letter is by Max Weber, the famous artist, from 1956, from America, from Long Island, written in English. I didn't find photographs. Books we have. He mentions here a book that he sent. So we probably have it. We have so many books that I didn't yet categorize, or do anything with them. But it's, again, part of the research that will be done, hopefully one day. This is the other side of the letter by Max Weber. This is Chaim Soutine. Chaim Soutine, as I learned, he's-- he was very famous. He didn't die because of the Nazis. He died because he was hiding from the Nazis, and he had a very terrible ulcer that killed him. So he was one of the victims of the Holocaust too. We don't have a letter by Soutine. We might have among the letters, that I cannot read the signature. This is one of nine letters that we have from Marc Chagall. That letter is from 1956, written in French, and signed by Marc Chagall. Another letter by Marc Chagall, again from 1956. He apologizes for starting that letter-- for writing that letter in French. And he says that this is easier for him because he has somebody that he can dictate to. But by the end of the letter, he wanted to write something personally. So he wrote in Yiddish on the side, as you can see. This is the famous by Marc Chagall. There is the Jew who is not at home. He is somewhere in the field with a book of-- with a scroll of the Torah. And near him is sitting this white cow with a violin, playing for him, making him a little bit happier. And in the sky is an angel flying. Sometimes he paints his daughter, Eda, as an angel. And this might be Eda.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the background, you see the houses. The houses are so blackish, as if there was a pogrom, and the Jew ran away from the disaster that-- that was running after him, sitting here and resting. But he had to save something. What did he save? Not the money, but the Torah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another painting by Marc Chagall. It's from the year 1928 or 1929. He wrote on the back 1928, and crossed the 8 and changed it to 9. So we are not sure when it was done. This is the backside of the painting that we just saw. And you can see the stamp of his agent. And you can also see the 28 or 29. He emphasized that this is 9. He really pressed on the pencil when he wrote that. What we saw was just a small part of the treasure, a real small part. There is so much more, but we couldn't show you more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is to become of Otto's treasure?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I just know that this is a treasure, and that there are so many artists that were completely eliminated, killed, destroyed. Their art was destroyed. And their name was destroyed. And we possess it. We have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about the painting that we're looking at right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a self portrait of Otto Schneid. He's trying to get out of the thorns. And he's pushing the thorns away. When we put this picture on the wall, about 2 years ago, we realized a very strange thing-- that wherever we went in the room, the eyes were looking at us. To any corner, the eyes were following us. And since then, we don't want to take this painting off. It's always on that spot. We worked today, I don't know, more than 12 hours. And I wanted to thank very much to these wonderful people, that you, Mr. Steven Spielberg, have a very rare, unique group of people. Starting with Roseanne, who came here twice, and spent hours talking and planning, and explaining, and calming down, and all that. And Janet, who came today. And she was like an angel looking after all the needs of everybody. And Tony, who is one of the best photographers that I could just imagine. God bless you for the huge thing that you are now doing, something so unique, that only Steven Spielberg can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to take the opportunity to thank you as well, for participating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very, very much. I'm going to read the poem that is on the grave of Otto, in the cemetery, Mount Sinai, which is on Wilson Avenue, in the [INAUDIBLE] section. There, a poem of his was inscribed in Hebrew. And this is an English translation.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hebrew, [HEBREW]. To those who will come after me. To those who come after, why all my fathers and mothers did give birth to you? Those who are blessed with vision and song, the dreamers of Jacob's Ladder, who know the earth and its secret, who hear the voice and its echo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Heavenly dwellers, martyred in destruction, burned at the stake, the mission of the first ones, the blessing of beginning upon you, and my love, and by love, I was fashioned into a link in this chain, and dedicated myself, and passed their blessing, to the maturing flowers. Take up courage. Brace yourselves to accept from my hand, this burning ember.
